items.title: Digital Devices: How every parent can master parental controls
items.description: By Josephine Agbonkhese You finally gave your child that smartphone or tablet, thinking it will help with school and staying connected with family and friends. But then the worries creep in. What if they stumble across stuff they shouldn’t see? How do you keep screen time from spiraling out of control? Read Also: 10 phones with best camera in 2026 The truth is, many people hand over these devices without really knowing how to set up proper safeguards. That’s where parental controls come in. Raising children in this always-online world feels tricky, doesn’t it? They rack up hours on their phones every day, and without boundaries, it can affect everything from sleep to academic performance, physical health and even self-esteem. So many parents also feel overwhelmed by the thought of cyberbullying, inappropriate content, mental health impact of constant social media comparison, or even strangers reaching out. Parental controls aren’t about playing Big Brother. They’re more like gentle guardrails that help guide your children while you teach them how to navigate safely on their own. They actually offer practical and effective solutions that don’t require you to become a full-time digital monitor. Start With Built-in Tools Wondering how to start? The great news is you probably already have solid options right on your devices. So, no extra apps are needed at first. If you’re in the Apple world with iPhones or iPads, head into Settings and look for Screen Time. Turn it on for your child’s device, and you can schedule Downtime for things like bedtime or study hours. Set limits on certain app categories so social media doesn’t eat up the whole day. You can even restrict content, block overwhelming social media apps, approve purchases, and control who they message. It feels pretty empowering once you get the hang of it. For Android families, Google Family Link is your friend. Download it on both phones, link the accounts, and suddenly you can manage daily time limits, filter what shows up in searches or YouTube, and even keep tabs on location if you need to. It also lets you restrict purchases, approve and completely block certain apps. Third-Party Apps Sometimes the basics do the job, but if you have a mix of devices or want deeper insights, many third-party apps step up nicely and also offer cross-platform support across iOS, Android, Windows, and even some smart TVs. These apps provide advanced capabilities such as real-time alerts for potentially dangerous keywords of cyberbullying language, website blocking, social media monitoring, and detailed activity timelines. Quite a number of them excel at scanning texts, emails, and social platforms for signs of cyberbullying or mental health concerns, notifying parents only when necessary to preserve privacy. Choosing the right app depends on your family’s specific needs and desired level of oversight. Use strong, unique passcode Be prepared though, children might try to outsmart you with clever workarounds or shared passwords. So, always use strong, unique passcodes and review settings regularly. The goal isn’t zero risk or creating a perfect digital routine but teaching them to make smart choices so they don’t need the controls forever. Don’t forget to lead by example. Model healthy tech habits by keeping phones away during dinner, prioritizing face-to-face family time, enjoying outdoor activities together, and discussing your own online choices openly with honesty and transparency. You don’t have to be a tech genius to start. Pick one feature this weekend, like setting a bedtime downtime, and build from there. Controlling Without Conflict So how do you actually use these without turning into the control freak parent? Start by sitting down with your child(ren) and explaining why you’re doing this. Frame it as protection and love, not punishment. Adjust settings based on their age. Younger children might need tighter control on games and social apps, while older ones can earn more freedom as they show responsibility. Check in together on the weekly reports. It turns into a great chance to chat about what they’re seeing online. The post Digital Devices: How every parent can master parental controls appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Customs releases impounded trucks after CGC’s intervention at bonded terminal
items.description: By Godwin Oritse The Nigeria Customs Service (NCS) has released 36 trucks belonging to members of the Association of Maritime Truck Owners (AMATO) that were impounded during an investigation into alleged trade infractions at Zarmmak Bonded Terminal in the Amuwo Odofin area of Lagos. The trucks were detained by Customs Police attached to the Zone ‘A’ Federal Operations Unit (FOU) after the terminal was sealed over suspected violations of Customs regulations. While the nature of the alleged infractions has not been disclosed by the Service, the incident disrupted trucking operations and raised concerns among transport operators over mounting business losses. The Secretary-General of AMATO, Mr. Sani Mohammed, confirmed the release of the trucks, attributing the development to the intervention of the Comptroller-General of Customs (CGC), Adewale Adeniyi, following repeated appeals by the Association. According to him, the affected truck owners maintained that they had no involvement in the alleged infractions under investigation and should not bear the consequences of actions linked to the terminal’s operations. “The intervention of the Comptroller-General has brought significant relief to our members who had suffered avoidable financial losses as a result of the prolonged detention of their trucks,” Mohammed said. In a statement, AMATO expressed appreciation to the Customs boss for what it described as a prompt and compassionate response that demonstrated the Service’s commitment to trade facilitation and stakeholder engagement. The association said the release of the trucks would enable affected operators to resume business and minimise the financial impact of the disruption. Commenting on the enforcement action, the Controller in charge of Zone ‘A’ Federal Operations Unit, Comptroller Aliyu Gambo, said the operation was carried out to ensure compliance with extant Customs laws. Efforts to obtain comments from the management of Zarmmak Bonded Terminal were unsuccessful, while calls and text messages sent to the FOU Controller before filing this report were not responded to. The post Customs releases impounded trucks after CGC’s intervention at bonded terminal appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: 11 poets compete for $100,000, ‘The Nigeria Prize for Literature 2026’
items.description: By Prisca Sam-Duru The Advisory Board of The Nigeria Prize For Literature, on Thursday, July 23, 2026, announced a longlist of 11 for the 2026 edition of the prize. The eleven outstanding poetry collections which emerged on the longlist were selected from 223 entries received earlier for the competition. The announcement, by the chairman of the Advisory Board for the Prize, Prof Akachi Adimora-Ezeigbo marks a significant milestone in this year’s competition and reflects the exceptional quality, creativity and diversity of contemporary poetry. The longlisted titles, arranged alphabetically are: Adult Love by Tanure Ojaide, Bakandimiyaby Saddiq Dzukogi, Black Passport by Paul Akpomuje, 2000 Blacks by Ajibola Tolase, Ceremony for The Nameless by Theresa Lola, and Corpus: Animistic Verses by Ayo Oyeku. Others are: Floral’s Love Colonyby Tares Oburumu, The Origin of Wounds by Malik Gbolahan, The Years of Blood by Adebayo Agarau, Unbind Me Nowby James Ugwu Eze and Why Does God Need a Gunby Ogaga Ifowodo. The announcement of the 11-title longlist formally opens the next phase of the shortlist of three in August and possible announcement of the winner in October. The Nigeria Prize for Literature, sponsored by NLNG, carries a cash award of $100,000 for the author of the winning book. In its 22nd year, the prize rotates annually across four genres – prose fiction, poetry, drama and children’s literature- with the 2026 edition devoted to poetry. Professor Adimora-Ezeigbo described the announcement as an important stage in the 2026 edition of the arguably African biggest and most prestigious literary Prize. She noted that the collections demonstrate the remarkable capacity of poetry to illuminate human experience through thoughtful reflection, cultural memory and artistic expression, adding that, the works revisit history while interrogating dominant historical narratives and exposing the forces that shape collective identities and social relations. Prof Adimora-Ezeigbo stated that despite their varied emphases, the books share a commitment to exploring the endurance of individuals and communities in the face of violence, oppression, and social fragmentation. Commenting on style and language, Prof. Adimora-Ezeigbo said the books display an impressive diversity of poetic techniques marked by lyrical intensity, symbolic depth, and artistic innovation. Her words, “Many employ densely poetic, allegorical, and elegiac modes that invite multiple layers of interpretation, while others draw extensively on folklore, oral traditions, and contemporary realities to create a compelling fusion of past and present. Their language is generally fluid, evocative, and aesthetically refined, relying on vivid imagery, emotional resonance, and intellectual sophistication to communicate complex ideas. These works demonstrate how poetic language can illuminate social realities; challenge established perspectives and give voice to both individual and collective experiences. “The next stage will demand a closer reading of each work, with attention to language, form, originality and lasting literary value.” She then commended the judges for their work and reaffirmed the Advisory Board’s commitment to a credible process, literary excellence and the promotion of a strong reading culture. The post 11 poets compete for $100,000, ‘The Nigeria Prize for Literature 2026’ appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Why Digital Trust Is Becoming Essential for Nigeria’s Growing SME Economy
items.description: For many small businesses in Nigeria, growth now happens partly on a phone screen. A fashion seller takes orders on Instagram. A food vendor receives payment before delivery. A small logistics operator confirms addresses through WhatsApp. A freelancer sends invoices to clients outside the country. The shop may still be physical, but the relationship with customers is increasingly digital. That shift has opened new doors for SMEs, but it has also raised a harder question: can customers trust what they see, pay for and share online? Trust Now Comes Before The Sale A few years ago, a buyer could walk into a shop, inspect a product and speak directly with the owner. That still happens, of course, but many transactions now begin with a post, a message, a payment link or an online catalogue. In that space, trust has to be built before the buyer ever meets the seller. This is why SMEs are paying closer attention to the tools and habits that support safer digital work. A business owner managing customer records, payments and online accounts may also look at practical security tools around daily operations. For example, users who work across Apple devices can install PIA on your Mac in a few minutes as part of a wider effort to manage access more carefully. For Nigerian SMEs, the main lesson is simple: customers are more likely to buy when the business feels visible, reachable and responsible. Online Payments Need Confidence Digital payments have helped many small businesses move faster. They reduce cash handling, make remote sales easier and allow customers to complete purchases without visiting a store. But payment convenience also brings fear. Failed transfers, fake alerts, phishing links and impersonation scams have made both buyers and sellers more cautious. That caution is not bad. In fact, it is part of a more mature digital economy. Businesses that confirm payments properly, use clear account details, protect customer information and communicate honestly stand out from those that treat online trust casually. A useful discussion by the World Economic Forum on digital trust explains why confidence in digital systems is becoming central to economic value. For SMEs, this is not an abstract issue. It affects whether a customer clicks, pays, returns or recommends the business to someone else. Reputation Travels Faster Online In Nigeria’s SME space, word of mouth has always mattered. What has changed is the speed. A good experience can bring referrals quickly. A bad one can spread just as fast. A delayed delivery, unclear refund process or unanswered complaint can damage a small brand in public. At the same time, consistent service, quick replies and transparent communication can help a business look more professional than its size suggests. That is why trust is now part of branding. It is not only about having a logo or posting regularly. It is about how the business behaves when a customer asks a question, makes payment or has a problem. Digital Growth Needs Better Habits Many SMEs are already using online tools, but not all have strong digital habits. Passwords are shared casually. Business and personal accounts mix together. Customer details sit in unsecured chats. Devices used for work are sometimes left unprotected. These small gaps can become expensive. A hacked account, lost customer data or fake payment message can hurt sales, reputation and daily operations. The solution does not always require complicated systems. It starts with discipline: stronger passwords, secure devices, clear payment processes, regular account checks and better staff awareness. A Foundation For The Next Stage Nigeria’s SME economy will keep growing through digital channels because customers are already there. They search, compare, message, pay and complain online. For small businesses, the opportunity is real, but so is the responsibility. Digital trust is becoming essential because it gives customers the confidence to engage. Without it, online visibility may attract attention, but not loyalty. The SMEs that build trust into their digital operations will be better placed to grow beyond their immediate streets, markets and communities. In a competitive economy, that trust may become one of their strongest assets. The post Why Digital Trust Is Becoming Essential for Nigeria’s Growing SME Economy appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Engineers seek stronger cost control, valuation to improve project delivery
items.description: By Obas Esiedesa Engineers under the Institute of Appraisers and Cost Engineers (IACE) have called for stronger application of engineering valuation, cost engineering and engineering economy principles to address cost overruns, waste and inefficiencies in project execution in Nigeria. The call was made at the 2026 National Technical Conference of the IACE, held in Abuja, with the theme: “Engineering Valuation, Cost Engineering and Engineering Economy Solutions in Emerging Economies.” The President of the Nigerian Society of Engineers (NSE), Engr. Ali Rabiu, said nations across the world were increasingly confronted with the challenge of maximising limited resources while meeting growing demands for infrastructure, economic growth and improved quality of life. Rabiu who was represented by Engr. Dauda Musa, said that in emerging economies such as Nigeria, engineering evaluation, cost optimisation and sound economic analysis had become indispensable to ensuring that public and private investments delivered maximum value and sustainable outcomes. He said the conference provided a unique opportunity for participants to exchange ideas, share experiences and explore innovative approaches to support the efficient management of scarce resources, accelerate infrastructure delivery and promote sustainable national development. In his speech, outgoing National Chairman of the Institute, Engr. Ike Iwenofu, said the combination of engineering valuation, cost engineering and engineering economy provided a framework for improving efficiency, reducing waste and maintaining tighter control over project budgets and timelines. The post Engineers seek stronger cost control, valuation to improve project delivery appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Osun Election: Senator Fadeyi quits APC, returns to PDP
items.description: …Says support for Tinubu, Renewed Hope Agenda remains unchanged By Shina Abubakar, Osogbo OSOGBO — Senator Olubiyi Fadeyi, representing Osun Central Senatorial District, has resigned from the All Progressives Congress (APC) and returned to the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP). Fadeyi was among the federal lawmakers who defected from the PDP to the APC but failed to secure the party’s senatorial ticket for the 2027 election. The APC ticket for Osun Central was won by Rasheed Adegoke (SAN), a development that sparked protests by some of Fadeyi’s supporters, who demanded that the ticket be returned to the incumbent senator. Unlike Fadeyi, other former PDP lawmakers who joined the APC, including Senator Francis Fadahunsi and members of the House of Representatives, Wole Oke, Taofeek Ajilesoro and Sanya Omirin, secured the party’s tickets to seek re-election. In his resignation letter, Fadeyi said his decision to leave the APC followed what he described as the party’s failure to honour an agreement that he would emerge as the consensus candidate for the Osun Central senatorial seat. He said he joined the APC in good faith to support President Bola Tinubu in his home state, believing assurances that he would receive the party’s ticket. “I believed those understandings would be honoured and that the party would uphold the principles of fairness, equity, mutual respect and internal democracy,” he said. The senator alleged that the process for selecting candidates departed from the agreed consensus arrangement and lacked transparency, leading to a loss of confidence in the party’s leadership in Osun State. He also expressed disappointment that while other lawmakers who defected with him secured return tickets, he was denied the opportunity to contest for another term. Despite his exit from the APC, Fadeyi stressed that his support for President Tinubu and the Renewed Hope Agenda remained unwavering. “I wish to state unequivocally that I remain one hundred per cent committed to President Bola Ahmed Tinubu and supportive of the Renewed Hope Agenda. My decision is not in opposition to the President, his administration or his vision for Nigeria,” he said. According to him, his decision was guided by what he described as the need to uphold democratic principles and protect the political mandate entrusted to him by the people of Osun Central Senatorial District. The post Osun Election: Senator Fadeyi quits APC, returns to PDP appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: The Bar Before the Bar, by Stephanie Shaakaa
items.description: We sit in air conditioned sanctuaries draped in the solemn austerity of black and white, parsing the fragile architecture of jurisprudence with the steady hands of secular priests. We cite sections and subsections like sacred scripture, penning eloquent briefs on the inviolability of the ballot, and standing before high tribunals to decry the systemic desecration of democratic choice. We posture as the vigilant guardians of the public trust, the ultimate safety net for a republic perpetually gasping for air. Yet when the robes are shed and the stage belongs to us alone, what tragic theatre do we enact? When the Nigerian Bar Association, an institution that has long presented itself as a guardian of national conscience and a defender of the rule of law, conducts its own internal ballot, the nation is confronted with a profound and chilling paradox. Collapsed e voting portals, locked out practitioners, ghost server glitches, and an unsettling scramble for legitimacy that threatens to drag the matter back toward the courts. It becomes a painful mirror held up to a profession that loves justice fiercely in theory, but risks undermining confidence in it through its own internal struggles. How does a learned elite find the moral courage to demand accountability from electoral bodies for technical failures and questions surrounding transparency, while its own election process leaves members questioning whether their voices were fully protected? How do senior advocates continue to sermonize on electoral integrity by day, only to confront difficult questions about transparency and confidence in their own electoral process by the dim light of election night? When the architects of jurisprudence construct houses of cards upon foundations of convenience, they inflict a wound far graver than ordinary electoral failure: they murder the public’s last remaining illusion. For generations, the standard response to Nigeria’s endless political tragedies has been a weary reassurance: take it to court, let the law speak. But what happens when the law’s own temple becomes the place where questions of trust are raised? What happens when the custodians of the keys find themselves locked in disputes over the very principles they have spent years defending? When the salt loses its savor, wherewith shall it be salted? This is the deeper contagion that threatens any institution built upon public confidence. It normalizes cynicism down to the marrow of society. It tells the ordinary citizen, watching from the margins, that integrity is merely a costume worn for public appearances, and that corruption and compromise are not political abnormalities but symptoms of a deeper national condition. When a professional body dedicated to equity and justice struggles to convince its own members that its transition of power reflects the transparency it demands from others, it releases a toxic vapor across the democratic landscape. It suggests that the sacred belief in self correction through institutions and ballots may be becoming an illusion shared only by those who still choose to believe. History is an unforgiving archivist, and it does not record the eloquence of our pleadings when our conduct mocks our prose. A Bar that cannot curate a transparent internal election places its moral authority to police the nation on trial. If the legal elite wishes to rescue the soul of the Nigerian ballot box, it must first sweep the debris from its own altar. Cleanse the temple, or risk losing the authority to claim that the house remains holy. The post The Bar Before the Bar, by Stephanie Shaakaa appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Los tres incendios que arrasan Madrid siguen ardiendo sin control: “Sopla mucho viento, va a ser un día muy peligroso”
items.description: Amanece el primer día en el que Madrid se encuentra en emergencia nacional a causa de los incendios forestales que asolan la región y la noche, aunque productiva en la lucha contra las llamas, no ha mejorado sustancialmente la situación. El Gobierno regional, que ya no está al mando del operativo al haber pedido anoche a La Moncloa que asumiera el control y haberlo decretado así el Ejecutivo, informa en su primer balance del día, a las siete de la mañana de este viernes, de que los trabajos nocturnos han sido “efectivos”, pero no se ha logrado ni perimetrar ni controlar los tres fuegos que arden simultáneamente desde el jueves, dos en Villa del Prado y uno en San Martín de Valdeiglesias . Ante la situación, España ha solicitado a Bruselas la activación del mecanismo europeo de respuesta y pide al menos cuatro aviones para luchar contra el fuego. Grecia ya ha aceptado la petición y ofrece dos aviones Canadair CL-415. Durante la mañana de este jueves se celebrará una reunión para preparar el despliegue. Seguir leyendo

items.title: Osun Election: Owa Obokun prays for APC candidate Oyebamiji, urges good governance
items.description: By Shina Abubakar, Osogbo OSOGBO — The Owa Obokun of Ijesaland, Oba Adesuyi Haastrup, has prayed for the governorship candidate of the All Progressives Congress (APC) in the August 15 election, Mr. Bola Oyebamiji (AMBO), expressing hope that God would grant him success and enable him to deliver good governance if elected. The monarch spoke on Thursday when Oyebamiji and members of the APC Governorship Campaign Council paid him a courtesy visit at his palace in Ilesa. Oba Haastrup stressed the importance of good governance and the benefits of cordial relations between a state government and the Federal Government. He cited the ongoing rehabilitation of the Ilesa-Ife-Ibadan Expressway as an example of the impact of such collaboration. The traditional ruler urged the APC candidate to support the development of Ijesaland, particularly the proposed Obokun University, if elected. “Our governor-to-be, Senator Francis Fadahunsi has started Obokun University. When you get there, whatever support you can give to make that dream a reality, please help us. “Your efforts will not be in vain. God will take you to the Promised Land. I know you will not forget Ijesaland because you are surrounded by patriotic Ijesa people,” the monarch said. Responding, Oyebamiji assured the monarch that, if elected, his administration would govern the state fairly and without bias. “Our government will not be guided by hypocrisy or nepotism. We will not concentrate the use of state resources in one community or town. We will govern Osun fairly and responsibly,” he said. Highlighting his record in public service, Oyebamiji recalled his tenure as Managing Director of the Osun State Investment Company Limited (OSICOL), where he said he revived the company and expanded its investment portfolio. According to him, OSICOL partnered with a company owned by the monarch to acquire properties for the state, including 32 four-bedroom flats in Guzape, Abuja, and additional properties in Ikoyi, Lagos. He also said the company established one of the largest filling stations in Osun State during his tenure without borrowing funds. “I revitalised OSICOL from a struggling company into a viable enterprise. We acquired valuable assets for Osun State and achieved these without borrowing a kobo,” Oyebamiji said. The post Osun Election: Owa Obokun prays for APC candidate Oyebamiji, urges good governance appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Africa’s next economic frontier will be built from already accumulated knowledge
items.description: Why Africa’s next economic frontier lies in transforming knowledge into institutions that enable enterprise and create shared prosperity. “The measure of development is not the number of projects we complete. It is whether what we learn becomes part of the institutions and enterprises that shape the future.” Africa’s next economic frontier will not be discovered beneath the ground. It will be built from the knowledge the continent has already accumulated. Across governments, entrepreneurs, researchers, farmers, universities and development institutions, Africa has spent decades generating practical knowledge about improving productivity, expanding markets, strengthening resilience and creating opportunity. The challenge before us is no longer discovering what works. Increasingly, we know what works. The more pressing question is whether we can convert that knowledge into institutions capable of attracting investment, enabling enterprise and ultimately creating shared prosperity. This conviction crystallised during a recent conversation with Uganda’s Head of State. Reflecting on Africa’s development journey, he challenged those of us working in development with a deceptively simple observation: do not try to become a government. Governments alone possess the legitimacy, reach and permanence required to scale transformation. Development institutions have a different responsibility. Their role is to generate evidence, demonstrate what works, convene partnerships and reduce uncertainty so that governments can institutionalise successful approaches and private enterprise can scale them sustainably. That observation has stayed with me because it redefines the purpose of development itself. It shifts our attention away from projects as the ultimate measure of success and towards the systems that survive long after projects have ended. For many years, development has understandably been organised around programmes and projects. They provide structure, mobilise resources and create opportunities to test new ideas. They remain indispensable because innovation requires experimentation, and experimentation inevitably involves risk. Yet the true value of a project is rarely found in its completion. It lies in the knowledge it generates and in whether that knowledge becomes embedded within institutions, influences public policy, informs investment decisions and enables enterprises to grow. This distinction matters because Africa is no longer a continent searching for solutions in the way it was several decades ago. Across agriculture, health, education, financial inclusion, climate resilience and governance, the continent has accumulated an extraordinary body of practical experience. Governments have implemented reforms. Researchers have generated evidence. Entrepreneurs have developed new business models. Farmers have adapted innovations to local conditions. Development organisations have invested billions of dollars in learning what succeeds under diverse African realities. The question is no longer whether Africa possesses knowledge. The question is whether Africa has built the institutional architecture required to convert that knowledge into long-term economic transformation. I would argue that knowledge should no longer be viewed as an output of development. It should be recognised as one of its most valuable forms of productive capital. Traditionally, development knowledge has been treated as something to be documented in reports, shared during conferences and archived at the conclusion of programmes. Yet knowledge behaves much like capital. Its value compounds when invested. It reduces uncertainty, improves decision-making, lowers the cost of innovation and creates confidence among governments, investors and entrepreneurs. Like infrastructure, it expands productive capacity. Like finance, it creates opportunities for future returns. Unlike many forms of capital, however, knowledge increases rather than diminishes when it is shared. Viewed through this lens, Africa’s greatest untapped asset may no longer be its natural resources, its agricultural potential or even its demographic dividend. It may be the accumulated knowledge the continent has yet to organise, institutionalise and deploy more intentionally. Reflecting on AGRA’s twenty-year journey reinforces this perspective. Beyond the projects implemented and partnerships established lies something potentially more enduring: a growing body of evidence about how agricultural transformation can succeed under different African contexts. That evidence has informed policy reforms, strengthened seed systems, expanded financial inclusion, improved market access and demonstrated practical approaches to increasing productivity and resilience. The lasting contribution of such work is not simply the immediate outcomes achieved during a project cycle, but the confidence it gives governments, investors and enterprises to adopt, adapt and scale proven solutions. This is perhaps where development institutions create their greatest value. They reduce uncertainty. They generate confidence. They demonstrate feasibility. They help societies move from assumption to evidence. Governments, in turn, perform a fundamentally different function. They institutionalise successful approaches through policy, regulation and public investment. Their legitimacy allows innovations to move beyond pilots and become part of national systems. Private enterprise performs yet another role. It commercialises innovation, mobilises investment, creates employment and sustains progress through competitive markets. None of these actors is sufficient on its own. Prosperity emerges only when each fulfils its complementary responsibility. Seen in this way, Africa’s development journey can be understood as a reinforcing progression rather than a collection of isolated interventions. Knowledge strengthens institutions. Strong institutions create confidence. Confidence attracts investment. Investment enables enterprise. Enterprise expands opportunity and productivity, creating the conditions for shared prosperity. The sequence is important because prosperity is rarely the consequence of a single intervention. It is the cumulative outcome of institutions, markets and leadership reinforcing one another over time. One implication follows naturally from this argument. Development projects should be designed with the end in mind. Their ultimate purpose is not to become permanent delivery mechanisms but to create conditions under which they are no longer required. Success should therefore be measured not only by outputs delivered or beneficiaries reached, but by whether institutions have acquired new capabilities, whether governments have adopted effective approaches, whether private enterprise has found commercially viable opportunities and whether communities are better positioned to sustain progress independently. This also requires us to think differently about knowledge itself. Roads, ports and digital infrastructure remain essential foundations of economic transformation, but Africa also requires a stronger knowledge infrastructure. The continent needs systems capable of capturing experience, preserving institutional memory and ensuring that successful ideas travel across borders, sectors and generations. Every successful intervention should strengthen Africa’s collective capacity to solve future challenges rather than requiring each new initiative to begin from the same starting point. The same principle applies to political leadership. Democratic transitions are an essential feature of healthy societies, but prosperity depends upon continuity of national purpose. Governments will rightly pursue different priorities, yet strong institutions enable each administration to build upon rather than replace the progress of those that came before. Development becomes cumulative when institutions preserve learning, creating confidence for citizens, investors and entrepreneurs alike. Narrative occupies an equally important place within this architecture. Too often, narrative is viewed primarily as a communications exercise. In reality, it performs an economic function. Narratives influence expectations, shape investor confidence and affect how societies perceive both risk and opportunity. They determine whether successful African innovations remain isolated examples or become recognised models worthy of replication and investment. Yet narratives derive their credibility not from optimism alone but from evidence. The most compelling story Africa can tell is one grounded in demonstrable progress and institutions capable of sustaining it. Agrifood systems provide one of the clearest opportunities to demonstrate this philosophy. Agriculture should no longer be viewed solely as a social sector or a poverty reduction instrument. Properly understood, agrifood systems connect production, manufacturing, finance, logistics, technology, retail and trade. They create enterprises, generate employment and stimulate industrialisation. When knowledge generated across these systems is effectively institutionalised and supported by investment, agriculture becomes not merely a source of food security but a driver of economic transformation. It is in this spirit that I begin The Prosperity Blueprint. The essays that follow are not intended to present a definitive prescription for Africa’s future. Rather, they seek to contribute to a conversation already emerging across governments, businesses, universities, development organisations and communities throughout the continent. They are an invitation to think more deliberately about the architecture required to convert knowledge into institutions, institutions into enterprise and enterprise into prosperity. Africa has spent decades demonstrating what is possible. Its next economic frontier will depend on whether it can transform that accumulated knowledge into capable institutions, confident markets and productive enterprises that create opportunity at scale. Prosperity, in the end, is neither accidental nor inevitable. It is built through deliberate choices, sustained learning and institutions capable of carrying one generation’s knowledge into the next generation’s opportunity. If this series contributes, even modestly, to that shared endeavour, it will have achieved its purpose. ******** The writer is the Chief Brand, Communications, External Engagements & Advocacy at AGRA.

items.title: Shettima pushes for stronger African voice in global digital policy
items.description: By Progress Godfrey Vice President Kashim Shettima has called for greater African influence in shaping global telecommunications and digital policies. He said that the continent must move beyond participation to actively driving decisions that will determine the future of digital technologies. Speaking at the High-Level Segment of the 7th Ordinary Session of the African Telecommunications Union (ATU) Conference of Plenipotentiaries (CPL-26) in Abuja, Shettima said Africa’s priorities have for too long been underrepresented in global technology governance. Represented by the Deputy Chief of Staff to the President, Senator Ibrahim Hadejia, the Vice President reaffirmed Nigeria’s commitment to regional digital cooperation, innovation and inclusive technology development. He said Africa must speak with a stronger and more coordinated voice in international digital policy discussions. “For too long, global technology policies have been developed without adequate representation of African priorities and perspectives. That must change,” he said. Shettima added that Nigeria would continue working through both the African Telecommunications Union (ATU) and the International Telecommunication Union (ITU) to ensure Africa plays a leading role in global digital decision-making. The conference, attended by ministers, regulators, industry executives and policymakers from the ATU’s 52 member states, is expected to deliberate on key issues including digital infrastructure, artificial intelligence, cybersecurity, spectrum management, digital skills development and Africa’s engagement in global digital governance. Minister of Communications, Innovation and Digital Economy, Dr. Bosun Tijani, said deeper collaboration among African countries had become imperative as digital technologies continue to transform economies across the continent. He announced the launch of the ATLAS Network, a continental initiative aimed at accelerating the development of African Large Language Models (LLMs). The initiative, which builds on Nigeria’s N-ATLAS programme, will bring together governments, universities, researchers, innovators and technology partners to develop AI systems that reflect Africa’s languages, cultures and knowledge systems. The post Shettima pushes for stronger African voice in global digital policy appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: US strikes Iran again to 'diminish threat to mariners, vessels' after attacks on Saudi oil tankers in Red Sea
items.description: US forces launched a fresh wave of strikes on Iran for the thirteenth consecutive day early on Friday after President Donald Trump vowed “major military punishment” for Tehran and its Houthi allies over attacks on two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea by the Yemeni rebels. The Houthi rebels, who control northern and western Yemen, said this week they were imposing a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia, which has diverted millions of barrels of oil per day by pipeline to the Red Sea to get around Iran’s blockade of Gulf oil through the Strait of Hormuz. Now, attacks in the Red Sea have extended the Middle East war to a second major shipping chokepoint. Fears that the disruption could widen to block another sea route sent global oil prices surging in one of the steepest rises of the war, which began with US-Israeli attacks on Iran in late February. Brent crude rose more than six per cent on Thursday, piercing the level of $100 a barrel for the first time since May. Early on Friday, Brent futures eased 72 cents, or 0.72pc, to $99.97 a barrel as of 0126 GMT, but remained on course for a 13.5pc advance this week. Houthi attacks could close the strait known as the Bab el-Mandeb or “Gate of Tears” which controls access from the Red Sea to the Indian Ocean, the second most important route for energy shipments after the Strait of Hormuz at the mouth of the Gulf. In the days since they unveiled their blockade in the Red Sea, several tankers have changed course to avoid the Bab el-Mandeb. Instead, they have headed north through the Suez Canal, potentially using a much longer and more expensive route to reach Asian customers by sailing around Africa. The number of tankers crossing the Strait of Hormuz fell to just one on Thursday, its lowest since May 7, shiptracking data showed. Fresh wave of strikes Two weeks since the effective collapse of an interim truce meant to end the war, the US military launched air strikes on Iran late on Thursday and early on Friday, for a 13th consecutive night of attacks. US Central Command (Centcom) said the latest strikes lasted more than two hours. “Centcom targeted Iranian military command centres, drone storage facilities, communication networks, coastal surveillance sites and maritime capabilities to further diminish the threat Iran poses to civilian mariners and commercial vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz,” it said in a statement. On both Thursday and Friday, Iranian state media said missiles struck Qeshm Island on the Strait of Hormuz, the virtually closed waterway that is the focus of a war well into its fifth month that has killed thousands and fanned fears of a global economic downturn. Four people were killed and five injured in a US missile attack on the Iranian city of Ahvaz, Iranian state broadcaster IRIB said. Iran’s semi-official Fars news agency reported that two people were injured following an early morning drone attack on Khorramabad. After the US strikes on Friday, Iran’s military said it had launched a new wave of drone attacks targeting US military facilities in Bahrain and Jordan. In a statement carried by Iran’s Mehr news agency, the Iranian army said it used drones to target fuel storage tanks, equipment warehouses, and troop living quarters at Sheikh Isa Air Base in Bahrain. The military added that aircraft hangars, maintenance facilities, and accommodation sites at the Al-Azraq base in Jordan were also hit. On Thursday, Iran said it attacked US missile systems, weapons and fuel storage sites in Jordan, as well as US military posts in Kuwait in retaliatory strikes. Iran’s Revolutionary Guards said they struck a US electronic warfare unit at Al-Adiri base in Kuwait, causing unspecified casualties, in response to an overnight strike on Iran’s Shalamcheh border crossing with Iraq. Neither the United States nor Kuwait has reported casualties at the base. Iranian military spokesperson Brigadier General Mohammad Akraminia said Iran would keep retaliating as long as the US continued to attack its infrastructure and coastal areas, Iranian state TV reported. Since the US and Israel began the war in February, Iran has demonstrated that it can hit US targets in the region, despite Trump claiming early on that the US had wiped out Iranian military capabilities. Trump says Iran hasn’t ‘received enough pain yet’ After the Houthis said they had struck the two tankers on Thursday, Trump said he would hold Iran accountable for any further attacks by the fighters. “If they do this again, the US will hold Iran responsible, in that the Houthis are a surrogate and/or proxy of Iran, and major military punishment will be inflicted upon Iran and, of course, the Houthis, themselves,” Trump wrote on social media. He told US news website Axios he was considering re-launching major combat operations in Iran and was close to a decision. “They haven’t received enough pain yet,” the news outlet quoted Trump as saying. On social media, Trump later said “any and all damages done” to cargo ships would be paid for with “Iranian Money,” referring to frozen Iranian assets held by the United States, but without saying how. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Arachi responded with a warning on X, saying, “Seizing another nation’s assets to pay for unrelated future claims is an incendiary precedent.” No one’s assets were safe once governments normalised such confiscation, he added on Friday. “Ensuing chaos will not be pretty or peaceful.” Rebuke by Congress High oil prices are stoking inflation globally, putting Trump’s Republican allies under pressure ahead of congressional elections in November. The Republican-led US House of Representatives backed legislation on Thursday directing Trump to halt US military action against Iran, for the latest rebuke by Congress, which has the exclusive power to “declare War” under the US Constitution. Repeated similar resolutions passed in recent months have not led to cessation of the war. The Senate voted hours later to block a separate, similar measure.

items.title: Christian Killings: Nigeria hiring lobbyists to mask insecurity — Ted Cruz
items.description: By Omeiza Ajayi Senator Ted Cruz of the United States of America, USA, has accused the President Bola Tinubu government of using public relations lobbying to mask killing of Christians in Nigeria. Read Also: 2027: PFN kicks against Muslim-Muslim ticket He expressed disappointment at Nigeria’s engagement of a lobbying firm to communicate its actions on protecting Christians in Nigeria to the US government, saying it was an attempt to deflect American concerns over the country’s security crisis. This came as former Deputy National Publicity Secretary of the All Progressives Congress APC, Timi Frank, alleged that the Presidency was deceiving Nigerians by presenting a reported letter from United States President Donald Trump as an endorsement of President Tinubu’s administration. The $9m lobbying firm Nigeria hired DCI Group, a US-based public affairs and lobbying firm, in a $9 million contract last December, few months after it was designated a ‘country of particular concern’ by President Donald Trump. According to the lobbying contract document filed with the US Department of Justice, Aster Legal, a law firm in Kaduna State, hired the services of DCI Group on behalf of the National Security Adviser, NSA, Nuhu Ribadu. US lawmakers have since introduced several measures, including bills aimed at pressuring the Nigerian government to address the insecurity crisis. Cruz, who had proposed the ‘Nigeria Religious Freedom Accountability Act of 2025’ in October, a legislation he said would hold Nigerian government officials accountable for “facilitating the mass murder of Christians” reiterated his stance in an X post on Wednesday. “Nigerian officials have created an environment that facilitates mass violence against Christians and religious minorities by imposing blasphemy and Sharia laws and looking the other way at jihadist atrocitiesd. “The U.S. government knows the Nigerian officials responsible for these policies, I have been pushing my bill to sanction them, and President Trump has even ordered military action. “Instead of responding to American concerns, Nigerian officials waged a public relations campaign against critics. They were confident in that campaign and in their ability to continue the status quo, and I am heartened that the House has just definitively proven them wrong.” Cruz was speaking in reaction to an amendment bill passed by the US House of Representatives that would increase withholding of assistance to Nigeria from 50 percent to 100 percent until the West African nation takes effective steps to prevent and respond to violence within its territory. Lawmakers agreed to the move with a voice vote last Wednesday. The senator is not the only lawmaker who has criticised Nigeria’s lobbying efforts. The same month, Nigeria hired DCI Group, Matthew Tonlagha, vice-chairman of Tantita Security Services, hired Valcour Global Public Strategy, a Washington-based lobbying firm, for the “purpose of strengthening the bilateral relationship” between the US and Nigeria. Chris Smith, House Foreign Affairs Africa subcommittee chairman, said the deals proved that “a culture of denial by Nigerian officials persists.” Tinubu’s govt deceiving Nigerians over Trump’s letter, Timi Frank alleges According to him, questions surrounding the release of court documents relating to President Tinubu’s alleged past drug-related case remained matters of public interest and should not be overshadowed by attempts to derive political capital from what he described as a routine diplomatic exchange. Frank, in a statement, in Abuja, yesterday, described the letter as a routine diplomatic correspondence acknowledging security cooperation between Nigeria and the US and challenged the Presidency to publish the document to prove its authenticity. “The letter is not from the White House. If it doesn’t have the White House seal and the signature of the President of the US, it appears dubious at best,” Frank said, questioning the origins of the document being celebrated by the Federal Government. “The letter only commended Nigeria for partnering with the US in addressing insecurity. That is the only reason President Trump may have written it. It was not an endorsement of President Tinubu’s leadership, his economic policies or his administration. “It did not say the Trump administration is backing President Tinubu or supporting him for any election in Nigeria. “The Presidency is only trying to change the narrative by presenting it as something more than it is,” he said. Frank, who is the United Liberation Movement for West Papua, ULMWP, Ambassador to East Africa and the Middle East, alleged that the publicity surrounding the reported correspondence was designed to divert public attention from ongoing discussions over the release and circulation of documents relating to President Tinubu’s alleged past drug-related matters. He pointed out that the letter was reportedly dated July 6, but only came into the public domain much later, arguing that if it truly represented a major political endorsement from President Trump, it would have been publicised immediately rather than surfacing weeks afterward. Frank recalled that former President Muhammadu Buhari was invited to the White House during Trump’s first term, arguing that such direct engagement carried far greater diplomatic weight than a reported letter. The former APC spokesman also questioned reports that the Federal Government had spent millions of dollars on lobbyists in the US to improve its international image, arguing that a government enjoying genuine international credibility would have little need for expensive reputation management abroad. “Ultimately, credibility and integrity cannot be conferred by a letter, a diplomatic exchange or a public relations campaign. They are earned through transparent leadership, accountability, respect for democratic institutions and faithful service to the people,” Frank said. Vanguard News The post Christian Killings: Nigeria hiring lobbyists to mask insecurity — Ted Cruz appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Airtel Africa dials up London for mobile money IPO
items.description: Airtel Africa said on Thursday it had picked the London Stock Exchange for the ​listing of its mobile money business ‌due later this year. Here are some details on the planned IPO and the firm’s first-quarter results: “We believe a ​London listing will provide access to ​a broad international investor base and support ⁠our ambition to unlock the long-term value ​of one of Africa’s leading fintech platforms,” CEO ​Sunil Taldar said. The African-focused telecoms group is targeting a listing of its third-largest unit Airtel Money in the second ​half of 2026, later than originally planned ​due to war-related cost pressures. The firm forecast pressure on its ‌near-term core profit margins for the second time in the last four months, citing higher energy costs arising from the Iran conflict. The Iran conflict ​has triggered ​a crude ⁠supply crunch and upended supply chains, raising energy and logistics costs ​for companies everywhere, and prompting forecast ​cuts, ⁠project delays, and cost-cutting drives. The company, however, reported higher first-quarter revenue and profits benefiting from strong ⁠demand ​for mobile networks, adoption of ​new digital technologies and artificial intelligence, and currency tailwinds.

items.title: Dollar to Naira exchange rate today, July 24, 2026
items.description: The Nigerian naira traded within a relatively stable range against the United States dollar on Friday, July 24, 2026, at both the official Nigerian Foreign Exchange Market (NFEM) and the parallel market, as traders continued to monitor foreign exchange liquidity and demand. Data from the official market showed the naira exchanging at approximately ₦1,369.92 to the US dollar at the NFEM, which serves as Nigeria’s official exchange rate benchmark. In the parallel market, commonly referred to as the black market, the dollar traded at around ₦1,420 per dollar, according to market trackers, although rates may differ slightly depending on location, dealer margins and transaction volumes. Based on the prevailing official rate, $100 would exchange for about ₦136,992, while $1,000 would be worth roughly ₦1.37 million at the NFEM. The spread between the official and parallel market rates remained relatively narrow compared with previous years, reflecting ongoing foreign exchange reforms and improved market liquidity, although analysts say demand for dollars from importers and other end-users continues to influence market movements. The Central Bank of Nigeria determines the official NFEM exchange rate using a volume-weighted average of transactions executed in the market, while parallel market rates are driven by demand and supply among currency dealers. The post Dollar to Naira exchange rate today, July 24, 2026 appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: At least 24 killed in bandit attack in northwest Nigeria, residents say
items.description: Armed bandits riding motorcycles killed at least 24 farmers ​and wounded several others in an ‌attack on remote farming villages in northwest Nigeria’s Zamfara state, residents told Reuters on Thursday. The attackers ​stormed communities in the Sauna district of ​the Talata Mafara local government area at about ⁠1300 GMT on Tuesday and opened fire on ​people working on their farms, residents told Reuters. “They ​killed at least 24 innocent farmers. We are still counting the losses,” resident Auwal Sani said by ​phone. Zamfara is at the centre of ​a security crisis in Nigeria’s northwest, where armed gangs locally ‌known ⁠as bandits frequently raid villages, kidnap residents for ransom and steal livestock. Residents said the gunmen travelled nearly 50 km (31 miles) along the ​Mayanchi-Anka highway, ​a key ⁠trade route, before reaching Sauna without encountering security forces. Police and military ​authorities were not immediately available for ​comment. Another ⁠resident, Bello Aliyu, said Tuesday’s attack lasted for about three hours. “They rustled an unspecified ⁠number ​of livestock, leaving several people ​wounded who are currently receiving treatment,” he said.

items.title: 2027: PFN kicks against Muslim-Muslim ticket
items.description: By Dapo Akinrefon Ahead of the 2027 general elections, the Pentecostal Fellowship of Nigeria, PFN, said it does not support the adoption of a Muslim-Muslim ticket for elective public office, saying Nigeria is remains a multi-religious and multi-ethnic nation. Read Also: 2027: Obi-Kwankwaso’s ticket dead on arrival —GANDUJE Recall that the All Progressives Congress, APC, has adopted President Bola Tinubu and his running mate, Vice President Kashim Shetimma, both Muslims, to contest the presidential election in 2027. However, while reacting to the move, the PFN, in a statement by its National Publicity Secretary, Dr Sylvanus Ukafia, after its NEC meeting, said the move does not reflect religious balance in the leadership of the country. The Christian body said: “The National Executive Council, NEC, of the Pentecostal Fellowship of Nigeria (PFN), at its duly convened meeting, has carefully considered recent developments affecting the nation and wishes to make the following resolutions known to the Nigerian public. “The PFN reiterates its unwavering commitment to the unity, peace, and progress of Nigeria. We remain convinced that our nation can only flourish where justice, equity, the rule of law, and respect for the Constitution prevail. “The PFN does not support the adoption of a Muslim-Muslim ticket for elective public office. Nigeria is a multi-religious and multi-ethnic nation whose diversity should be reflected in its leadership. Inclusive governance promotes national unity, mutual trust, and a sense of belonging among all citizens.” On the proposed law restricting Christian preaching, the PFN said it “strongly opposes any law or policy that seeks to ban or unduly restrict Christian preaching.” Such a law, it said is “inconsistent with the provisions of the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, which guarantees every citizen the freedom of thought, conscience, religion, expression, and peaceful assembly.” It noted that “while the PFN supports lawful regulation necessary to preserve public order, such regulation must never become an instrument for suppressing legitimate religious expression or infringing upon fundamental human rights. “We respectfully call upon all arms and levels of government to uphold the Constitution, protect the rights of every Nigerian regardless of faith, and ensure that no legislation discriminates against any religious community”, it stated. It, however, urged Christians across the country to remain “peaceful, prayerful, law-abiding, and steadfast. We must continue to demonstrate the love of Christ while exercising our constitutional rights responsibly and without violence or provocation.” “We encourage sincere dialogue among government, religious leaders, and civil society in addressing issues capable of threatening our national cohesion. Nigeria’s diversity should be regarded as a strength to be nurtured rather than a source of division. “The PFN remains commited to praying for our nation and working with all stakeholders to build a peaceful, just, and prosperous Nigeria where every citizen enjoys equal protection under the law.” The post 2027: PFN kicks against Muslim-Muslim ticket appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: UN rights office deplores alleged killing, torture of Malian soldiers by insurgents
items.description: The U.N. human rights office on Thursday called for an investigation into reports of the torture and killing of surrendering Malian soldiers during an ​ambush of a military convoy by insurgents and separatists, saying such actions ‌would amount to a war crime. The ambush occurred on Saturday in northern Mali between the town of Anefis and the city of Gao, according to statements from Mali’s armed forces, the Azawad Liberation ​Front (FLA), and the al Qaeda-linked group Jama’at Nusrat al-Islam wal Muslimin (JNIM). A ​source close to the FLA told Reuters that more than 50 ⁠pro-government fighters were killed. Reuters could not independently confirm the death toll. JNIM has ​shared footage online that appeared to show soldiers surrendering following the ambush, with their ​hands behind their heads, as well as rebels firing at some of their captives. Reuters has not independently verified the footage. “There needs to be a thorough and independent investigation into the circumstances ​of this incident, including the actions contained in the horrendous footage circulated online appearing ​to show FLA/JNIM members firing at the soldiers after they surrendered,” Thameen Al-Kheetan, spokesperson for the ‌Office ⁠of the U.N. High Commissioner for Human Rights, said in a statement on Thursday. The office deplores “the reported torture and killing of dozens of surrendering Malian soldiers,” he said. JNIM could not be reached for comment. A spokesperson for the FLA, Mohamed Elmaouloud ​Ramadane, said the ​group supported independent ⁠investigations of all human rights abuses committed in the region and that the footage described by the U.N. human rights office “must ​first be authenticated”. JNIM and the FLA staged nationwide attacks in April ​that killed ⁠the defence minister, hit the airport and forced the army to abandon several locations. They followed that with an attempt on July 4 to seize Anefis, though the ⁠army has ​retained control of that town. The mounting violence poses ​a threat to Mali’s military-led government, which took power in coups in 2020 and 2021 and promised to ​improve security in the landlocked Sahel country.

items.title: Osun poll: Accord Party, Police differ over officers’ neutrality
items.description: By Shina Abubakar, OSOGBO AHEAD of the August 15 governorship election in Osun State, the Accord Party and Police Command, yesterday, traded accusations over the neutrality of security agencies in the state. The Chairman of the Accord Party, Pastor Victor Akande, accused the Osun Police Command of providing cover for suspected thugs, while members of rival groups, particularly transport union members, were subjected to harassment and selective arrests. Akande urged the Inspector-General of Police, IGP Tunji Disu, to order an impartial investigation into the activities of all individuals linked to the recent violence in the state and ensure that anyone found culpable was prosecuted regardless of political affiliation. However, Police Public Relations Officer, DSP Abiodun Ojelabi, dismissed the allegations, describing them as false and misleading, maintaining that it remained committed to professionalism, impartiality and the rule of law. He said it had never provided protection for any individual, political party or criminal group, stressing that its operations were intelligence-driven and based strictly on credible evidence. Ojelabi said: “Arrests were not carried out based on media reports, political statements or social media allegations, but on evidence capable of sustaining prosecution in court. Several suspects linked to breaches of public peace had already been arrested, investigated and charged where sufficient evidence existed, while investigations into other reported incidents were ongoing. “Some of the arrested suspects had confessed to plans to procure arms and ammunition to perpetrate further violence.” The post Osun poll: Accord Party, Police differ over officers’ neutrality appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Obasanjo to Osoba: ‘I didn’t deceive AD leaders in 2003’
items.description: By James Ogunnaike, ABEOKUTA FORMER President Olusegun Obasanjo, yesterday, faulted claims made by former Ogun State Governor, Aremo Olusegun Osoba, that he deceived leaders of the Alliance for Democracy, AD, during the political realignments leading to the 2003 general elections, insisting that he neither misled anyone nor acted in bad faith. The former President, in a letter dated July 23, 2026, titled ‘Reply to your inaccurate posture: ‘How Obasanjo deceived us in 2003’, described Osoba’s account, published in The Legal Observer on July 20, as “farfetched” and riddled with inaccuracies, omissions and misconceptions. Obasanjo took particular exception to Osoba’s allegation that he deceived Yoruba political leaders, insisting that his political engagements had always been guided by openness and national interest. He said: “I take strong and serious objection to your calling me a deceiver because I deceived nobody. I always presented my case and all issues as I had them and as I understood them, no matter whose ox is gored.” Obasanjo argued that several political developments preceding his emergence as President in 1999 were unknown to Osoba because they were handled by senior leaders of Afenifere and the AD. Recalling his engagements with the late Afenifere leader, Chief Abraham Adesanya, Obasanjo said despite being repeatedly told that Yoruba people would not vote for him, he continued to maintain cordial relations with the elder statesman. He said: “There was no time I called on Papa Adesanya that I was not well entertained. Yet Papa stood on no vote for me, and I stood on my respect and what was due to him. “Bringing PDP and AD to join me for a sort of national government was not out of weakness; rather, it was out of strength and for the unity and progress of the country.” He also rejected Osoba’s account of events surrounding the attempted impeachment of former Speaker of the House of Representatives, Mr Ghali Na’Abba, alleging instead that the impeachment plot originated from within his administration. He said: “The game continued to be played on with me treating it with contempt as I had committed no impeachable offence.” On the 2003 elections, Obasanjo disputed Osoba’s suggestion that the defeat of AD governors resulted from political manipulation by the Presidency. He said: “Your loss or defeat in 2003 was genuine in all the six states except the one where the figures were successfully and murderously changed. I have never believed in tribal politics.” While criticising Osoba’s interpretation of events, the former President acknowledged the former governor’s contributions to Ogun State and to the establishment of the Olusegun Obasanjo Presidential Library, OOPL, in Abeokuta. He commended Osoba for allocating the land on which the library stands, describing it as a landmark project that has benefited the state. “The greatest service you rendered for me is the land allocated by you for the Olusegun Obasanjo Presidential Library. It has become the most significant landmark in Abeokuta. Politics may end, relationships may live till life’s end, there is no end to humility and thanksgiving”, he said. The post Obasanjo to Osoba: ‘I didn’t deceive AD leaders in 2003’ appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Where is the man who put us in debt? – Majority Leader demands Ofori-Atta’s return
items.description: Majority Leader Mahama Ayariga has called for former Finance Minister Ken Ofori-Atta to return to Ghana to account for what he described as the country’s worsening debt burden under the previous New Patriotic Party (NPP) administration. Contributing to the debate on the 2026 Mid-Year Fiscal Policy Review in Parliament on Thursday, July 23, Mr Ayariga accused the former Finance Minister of recklessly borrowing and mismanaging the economy before leaving the country. “Where is the man who put us in debt? Where is Ken Ofori-Atta? After borrowing recklessly and creating the debt, he has gone and is hiding in the United States of America. We must bring Ken Ofori-Atta back,” he said. The Majority Leader argued that Mr Ofori-Atta should be made to explain to Ghanaians how the country’s finances were managed during his tenure. “Ken Ofori-Atta should be brought back to account to the good people of Ghana for why he so grossly mismanaged our finances and left ordinary Ghanaians to struggle to fix the economy while he enjoys himself in the United States of America,” he added. Mr Ayariga contrasted the performance of the current Finance Minister, Dr Cassiel Ato Forson, with that of his predecessor, claiming the economy is now being managed without imposing burdensome taxes or engaging in excessive borrowing. “We were in this country, in this Parliament, and there was a Finance Minister who told us that he could not run this economy without taxing our mobile money transfers. “Today, we have a Finance Minister who has abolished all those taxes, and yet he is successfully managing our economy. We have a Finance Minister who is not borrowing recklessly, but is successfully managing our economy,” he stated. The Majority Leader commended Dr Forson for what he described as prudent fiscal management, saying the Finance Minister had demonstrated sound leadership through policies aimed at stabilising the economy while easing the tax burden on citizens. He also criticised the previous NPP administration over its spending priorities, arguing that the current government is investing in critical infrastructure, including schools, roads and bridges, instead of projects such as the National Cathedral. According to Mr Ayariga, the 2026 Mid-Year Budget Review reflects government’s commitment to expanding educational infrastructure, rehabilitating schools and undertaking major road projects across the country. He also expressed confidence in Dr Forson’s stewardship of the economy, declaring that “Ghana is working again” under the current administration and urging Parliament to commend the Finance Minister for his performance.

items.title: Stakeholders back bill requiring social media firms to open offices in Nigeria
items.description: Support proposed AI Academy in Ekiti By Henry Umoru ABUJA — Stakeholders have expressed support for a bill seeking to compel social media platforms operating in Nigeria to establish physical offices in the country. They also endorsed a separate bill proposing the establishment of an Artificial Intelligence (AI) Academy in Omuo-Ekiti, Ekiti State. The support was expressed on Thursday during a public hearing organised by the Senate Committee on ICT and Cyber Security on two bills before the Senate. The first bill, titled A Bill for an Act to Amend the Nigeria Data Protection Act, 2023, to Mandate the Establishment of Physical Offices within the Territorial Boundaries of the Federal Republic of Nigeria by Social Media Platforms, and for Other Related Matters, 2025 (SB. 650), is sponsored by Senator Ned Nwoko (APC, Delta North). The second bill, sponsored by Senator Yemi Adaramodu (APC, Ekiti South), seeks to establish an Artificial Intelligence Academy in Omuo-Ekiti. Chairman of the Senate Committee on ICT and Cyber Security, Senator Shuaib Afolabi Salisu (APC, Ogun Central), said the social media bill was aimed at strengthening Nigeria’s digital ecosystem, while the AI Academy bill would create a centre of excellence for AI education and research. Declaring the hearing open, Senate President Godswill Akpabio, represented by Deputy Senate Leader, Senator Lola Ashiru (APC, Kwara South), described both bills as forward-looking legislative proposals that reflected the Senate’s commitment to strengthening governance and technological development. Akpabio said the proposed requirement for social media companies to establish offices in Nigeria was not intended to stifle their operations but to improve engagement with regulators and users. Speaking at the hearing, Senator Nwoko said the bill was neither hostile to innovation nor designed to discourage investment. “This Bill is not punitive. It seeks to deepen the engagement of global technology companies with Nigeria by encouraging them to become responsible corporate citizens,” he said. According to him, countries including the United Kingdom, Ireland, Singapore, India, Brazil, South Africa and Japan have attracted investments from major technology firms through local operations that generate jobs, improve regulatory compliance and encourage technology transfer. He argued that, as Africa’s largest digital market, Nigeria should enjoy similar benefits. Nwoko said the proposed legislation would require social media platforms, data controllers and data processors operating in Nigeria to maintain functional offices within the country. He added that local offices would strengthen national security by providing direct channels of engagement between technology companies and Nigerian authorities, improve enforcement of data protection laws, enhance consumer protection, increase tax compliance and create employment opportunities for Nigerians. The senator also said the bill would promote technology transfer through collaboration between multinational technology firms and Nigerian professionals, startups and educational institutions. He maintained that the legislation was intended to ensure accountability and align Nigeria with global best practices. Nwoko recalled that the bill, which passed second reading in March, was introduced in response to the absence of physical offices of major social media companies, including Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, X, YouTube, TikTok and Snapchat, despite their extensive operations and user base in Nigeria. He noted that the bill also proposes measures to improve accountability in Nigeria’s digital media space, including provisions affecting bloggers operating in the country. According to him, the legislation seeks to promote professionalism, transparency and responsible digital engagement while ensuring that multinational technology companies contribute more directly to Nigeria’s economy and regulatory framework. The post Stakeholders back bill requiring social media firms to open offices in Nigeria appeared first on Vanguard News .

items.title: Oxford No. 1 Hotel rejects receivership claims, says facility remains under management’s control
items.description: Management of Oxford No. 1 Hotel has dismissed reports that the facility has been taken over by a receiver appointed by Cola Holdings Ltd, insisting that the hotel remains under the possession and control of Kensington Residential Partners 1 Limited (KRP1 Ltd). In a statement issued on Thursday, July 23, management said the hotel continues to operate under its directors and management, urging staff, customers and the general public to disregard claims that a receiver has assumed control of the facility. According to the statement, Oxford No. 1 Hotel was developed and is managed by KRP1 Ltd, a company jointly owned by its directors, Nana Kwame Bediako, popularly known as Cheddar, and Azad Cola. Management explained that the project was financed through shareholder capital and a loan from the International Finance Corporation (IFC), but the COVID-19 pandemic disrupted operations after the hotel opened in December 2019, affecting its ability to service the facility. Management alleged that while KRP1 Ltd was negotiating with the IFC to restructure the loan, Mr Cola, through Cola Holdings Ltd, informed the company that he had personally settled the outstanding debt and subsequently demanded reimbursement. It argued that as a director of KRP1 Ltd, Mr Cola had a fiduciary duty to obtain the company’s approval before taking such action and further claimed that neither he nor Cola Holdings Ltd had produced evidence showing that the IFC loan had been fully repaid. The statement said the dispute escalated after Cola Holdings Ltd secured a High Court order on July 21, 2026, appointing a receiver to take possession of the hotel. However, management maintained that although the order authorises the Ghana Police Service to assist with enforcement, it does not take effect until seven days after it was granted. KRP1 Ltd said it has instructed its lawyers to appeal the decision and seek an injunction restraining Cola Holdings Ltd and the appointed receiver from taking possession of the hotel pending the determination of the appeal. It added that two earlier applications filed by Cola Holdings Ltd had been dismissed by the High Court and remain under appeal. Management further alleged that Cola Holdings Ltd has instituted separate legal proceedings against Nana Kwame Bediako personally to recover the same amount it claims to have paid to the IFC while simultaneously seeking repayment from KRP1 Ltd. According to the statement, this amounts to an attempt to recover the same debt twice, which it believes the courts will not permit. Oxford No. 1 Hotel assured customers and stakeholders that its operations remain uninterrupted and urged the public to await the outcome of the ongoing legal proceedings.

Il a devancé le Suisse Mauro Schmid et l'Américain Matteo Jorgenson, arrivés 45 secondes après lui.
